# \# **Week 8** SAR or temperature, or combination

Loaded and displayed in GEE and researched the SAR properties for Identifying different in structural bodies using active sensor reluctance for heat identification in urban areas. Linking the practical application with past studies and theory

Whilst multispectral sensors passively measure electromagnetic radiation reflected from the Earth's surface, radar sensors are active, meaning they emit electromagnetic radiation and then measure the returning signal. Multispectral sensors capture information on chemical properties of surfaces, such as nitrogen or carbon content and moisture (Asner, [**1998**](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0002 "https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0002"); Tempfli, Kerle, Huurneman, & Janssen, [**2009**](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0104 "https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0104")), 

**Practical – Week 8** This week focused on splitting the Level‑3 boundaries and adding a mask to the data before loading it, as well as converting the temperature data from Kelvin to Celsius. This was of use in understanding the group work to explaine conversion of raw data to a simplified measure.

The code loaded and ran successfully, producing an impressive output in both coverage and granularity. The cooler appearance of the rivers immediately increased confidence in the imagery. Colour parameters were taken from MODIS.

The resulting image is somewhat blurry and less spatially precise, but it provides strong areal representation of warmer and cooler regions. This is because the MODIS collection contains more images to draw temperature data from. The output is visually appealing and useful for comparing different regions, as well as for higher‑level policy perspectives.

This week highlighted the importance of interpreting images and understanding how they are captured, processed, and produced. The best sensor to use depends entirely on the specific question being asked. For flooding as an example it is not easy to identify. Any surface that is large and smooth can appear as water in SAR imagery due to specular reflectance, leading to misclassification. Examples include airports, exposed channel beds, and highways. Seasonal flooding—regular, *expected annual flooding*—must also be considered.

And there are muti sensor satalites that can capture the vertical column measurements of NO2, for example. The troposphere is the closest we can get to ground-level measurements with Sentinel-5P. (Zander, Venter,Sourangsu, Chowdhury)

There is a clear need for training in image interpretation, as well as in loading, manipulating, and understanding imagery. Further reading and analysis will help answer both existing and emerging questions.

An area of interest is whether ancient buildings in Egypt—and associated mining works or structures—could be identified. This would require understanding what features to look for before searching the imagery, unless code were developed to detect rubble signatures, columns, or other characteristic pixel patterns.

![](images/clipboard-315653524.png)
